title: Oxtail and butter beans
subtitle: Slow-braised Jamaican oxtail with a rich, gelatinous gravy
excerpt: Oxtail pieces braised for three hours in a spiced gravy with allspice, scotch bonnet, and thyme, finished with butter beans that absorb the deep, sticky sauce. A weekend dish that rewards patience.
sourceNotes: Oxtail stew is a cornerstone of Jamaican cooking and one of the most widely eaten dishes in the Jamaican diaspora. The long braise breaks down the collagen in the oxtail into gelatin, giving the gravy an almost sticky quality that no other cut can replicate. Butter beans are added towards the end and are cooked until they absorb the gravy and become dense and flavourful. The dish is distinct from European oxtail preparations in its use of allspice, scotch bonnet, and the browning technique common to Jamaican stew cooking.

content.text: Oxtail is the dish Jamaican cooks make on Saturdays because it takes the whole day. Three hours at the lowest possible heat is the minimum; some cooks go four. The collagen in the oxtail gradually breaks down, turning the braising liquid into a thick, glossy gravy that coats the back of a spoon and clings to the rice. Butter beans go in for the last 30 minutes and turn dense and savoury, absorbing the gravy entirely.

The browning step is non-negotiable. Sugar melted in hot oil to a deep amber, then the oxtail pieces turned in it, gives the gravy its colour and a faint bitter-caramel undertone that you won't get from just searing in plain oil. Don't rush this stage and don't crowd the pan.

items.name: Oxtail
items.prepNote: cut into sections through the joints, ask the butcher

content.text: Toss the {{oxtail}} of oxtail pieces with {{allspice}} of allspice, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper. Leave for 20 minutes.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a large Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Add the {{dark-brown-sugar}} of sugar and let it melt and bubble to a deep amber, about 2 minutes. Add the oxtail pieces in batches and turn to coat each piece in the caramel, browning for 3 to 4 minutes per batch. Work in batches so the sugar doesn't drop in temperature. Remove to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onions and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{spring-onion}} spring onions, and {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme; cook for 2 minutes. Stir in the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Return the oxtail to the pot and add enough water to come halfway up the meat, roughly 600 ml. Add the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and cook over the lowest possible heat for 2.5 hours, turning the oxtail once halfway through.

Add the {{butter-beans}} of butter beans, stir gently, and continue cooking uncovered for 30 minutes until the beans are heated through and the gravy has thickened to a glossy consistency. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme sprigs. Taste for salt and serve.

items.fix: Continue cooking in 30-minute increments. The meat should give easily when pressed and fall away from the bone at the joint. Four hours is not unusual for very large pieces
items.cause: Individual pieces vary; larger sections take longer

items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat to medium for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the gravy coats the back of a spoon
items.cause: Too much water added initially; lid not tight enough
items.symptom: Gravy very thin after the full cook

items.fix: Add them in the final 20 minutes only, at a gentle simmer. Butter beans are very tender and fall apart quickly in acidic or hot liquids
items.cause: Added too early or cooked at too high a heat

items.fix: Check salt levels carefully after the long cook. A dash of Worcestershire sauce at the end adds the depth the dish needs
items.cause: Insufficient seasoning or Worcestershire sauce omitted

content.text: Oxtail and butter beans is one of those dishes that signals a genuine occasion in Jamaican cooking. It takes the whole afternoon and fills the kitchen with a smell that can't be rushed. The cut was once considered a cheap by-product, but the long slow braise extracts everything from it: the marrow enriches the gravy, the collagen gives it body, and the meat falls from the bone in thick, sticky shreds. Serve with rice and peas. The gravy is the reason to be there.
